title: Mommy Meyer

jokes.text: Please don't make me go to work today. I'll fake my own death.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about the pressures of being president
jokes.punchline: Her solution to avoiding work is to fake her own death — absurdly disproportionate to the problem
jokes.explanation: A solid cold-open gag establishing Selina's fundamental unsuitability for her own job. The escalation from 'I don't want to go' to 'I'll fake my own death' is character-specific and efficiently delivered. Not exceptional but does the job of immediately re-establishing Selina's voice.

jokes.text: You don't have the cheekbones for depression.
jokes.setup: Selina says she'll fake her own death, implying she's miserable
jokes.punchline: Gary's response rejects the premise not on emotional grounds but on a wildly irrelevant aesthetic criterion — cheekbones
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines of the episode. Gary's sycophantic worldview — where everything is filtered through Selina's appearance and image — is perfectly captured in a single absurd non-sequitur. It's original, economical, completely character-specific to Gary, and earns its laugh through genuine wit rather than any shortcut. Highly quotable and would land hard in a room.

jokes.text: It's falling apart like a punched wedding cake.
jokes.setup: Selina describes the Families First Bill falling apart politically
jokes.punchline: The simile 'like a punched wedding cake' — vivid, violent, and oddly specific
jokes.explanation: A signature Veep-style invented simile. The image is immediate and absurd — a wedding cake being punched is both comically violent and strangely apt for political collapse. Strong originality and economy. Slightly below the very top tier only because it's a one-off image rather than having deeper structural resonance.

jokes.text: The press has recently decided to call it the Mommy Meyer Bill.
jokes.setup: The president wanted the bill called the Meyer Bill to boost her brand
jokes.punchline: The press instead dubbed it the 'Mommy Meyer Bill,' undermining the gravitas she sought
jokes.explanation: The setup for the episode's recurring theme. Functional comedy of humiliation — Selina's brand-building backfired spectacularly. Gets the job done as exposition but doesn't transcend it.

jokes.text: Take this fork, stab me right here in the carotid.
jokes.setup: Selina has just catalogued her mounting problems
jokes.punchline: Her hyperbolic response is to request precise anatomical self-stabbing, adding grotesque specificity ('carotid') to the standard 'kill me' trope
jokes.explanation: A step above the generic 'kill me' complaint because of the medical specificity ('carotid') which signals Selina's controlled, precise personality even in despair. Solid Veep voice.

jokes.text: You are the most influential Mike person in the world.
jokes.setup: Someone is giving Selina a pep talk about her influence
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'Mike person' is bizarre and deflating — she's the most influential person named Mike? The superlative collapses on itself
jokes.explanation: This appears to be a subtitle error ('Mike' likely means 'influential') but as written it reads as an odd deflating qualifier. If intentional, it's a quiet absurdist beat; if a transcription error, the comedy is accidental. Scored conservatively.

jokes.text: The next one's foreign policy. That's basically a quiz on the entire world.
jokes.setup: There are two more presidential debates and the next is foreign policy
jokes.punchline: Describing a foreign policy debate as 'basically a quiz on the entire world' — reductive and comic in its understatement of what governance requires
jokes.explanation: A decent observational line about the absurd scope of what politicians are expected to know. Competent but not standout.

jokes.text: Oh, shit. Happy birthday. I'm sorry I didn't have time to get you anything. Gonna try after work.
jokes.setup: Selina's pep talk ends and she's about to leave, clearly in presidential mode
jokes.punchline: She suddenly remembers it's someone's birthday — after the conversation has ended — and her consolation is 'gonna try after work,' treating a personal gesture like a work task
jokes.explanation: A small but effective cringe moment. Selina's inability to operate as a normal human being — forgetting a birthday, treating emotional obligations like calendar items — is core character comedy. 'Gonna try after work' is the key deflation.

jokes.text: I live on a regular street in real America with real trees and real cars. Oh, wait for it. We do have real cars on my street. I'm not sure what a fake car would be exactly.
jokes.setup: A politician is performing folksy authenticity on TV, claiming to live in 'real America' with 'real cars'
jokes.punchline: The self-aware qualifier 'I'm not sure what a fake car would be exactly' punctures the performative authenticity
jokes.explanation: Sharp media satire — the politician's hollow 'real America' rhetoric is exposed by its own internal absurdity. 'What's a fake car?' is the right question to ask and the show earns it.

jokes.text: Oh, bam! I should be president or something.
jokes.setup: Selina's team is watching what appears to be political coverage and she's pleased with something
jokes.punchline: 'I should be president or something' — delivered by the sitting president, making the ambition sound still tentative and aspirational
jokes.explanation: A clean irony gag: the president saying 'I should be president or something' captures her perpetual outsider complex even from the Oval Office. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: If he doesn't drink in the morning, he will now.
jokes.setup: They're watching a political opponent on TV get blindsided by something
jokes.punchline: The casual observation that the footage will drive a man to morning drinking — delivered as a matter of fact
jokes.explanation: A functional dark quip. Gets a small laugh from the casual cruelty but not particularly original.

jokes.text: That rumor about O'Brien's daughter blowing all those hockey players in college. That would have been a mistake. Huge mistake. It was lacrosse players.
jokes.setup: Selina regrets not bringing up a scandalous rumor about O'Brien's daughter with hockey players
jokes.punchline: The correction isn't 'that would be wrong to say' — it's that the sport was lacrosse, not hockey. The moral problem is completely bypassed; only the factual error matters.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional Veep joke. The structure is perfect misdirection: the audience expects the moral corrective ('you shouldn't say that') and instead gets a factual corrective (wrong sport). It's character-specific — these people are not capable of ethical objection, only strategic objection — and the escalation from 'huge mistake' to 'it was lacrosse players' is pitch-perfect. Highly quotable and a genuine standout.

jokes.text: Gang? Did you all have tattoos? How did you get into this gang? Did you have to kill a guy? You could pardon yourself now.
jokes.setup: Selina refers to her old lawyer friends as 'the gang' — meaning a social group
jokes.punchline: Jonah and/or Gary take 'gang' literally, escalating through gang-membership tropes (tattoos, killing to join) before Gary offers the presidential pardon as a solution
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed escalating bit. The pardon callback at the end ('you could pardon yourself now') is the best part — it applies presidential power to a completely absurd premise. Good structural instinct.

jokes.text: Plus we had tits and ass. So we had the whole thing going for us, really.
jokes.setup: Selina is reminiscing about how she and her lawyer friends dominated Annapolis in the '90s as empowered women
jokes.punchline: She adds their physical assets to the list of professional qualifications — delivered without irony, as if it's simply another achievement
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy — Selina's unselfconscious use of sexual capital as just another tool is both funny and revealing. The feminist framing of the previous sentence makes the pivot land harder.

jokes.text: There's been a shooting in Pittsburgh. Four dead including the gunman. Well, that's fucking not good.
jokes.setup: A shooting has just been reported — a genuinely tragic event
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is the most miniaturized possible reaction: 'that's fucking not good' — the profanity amplifying rather than softening the gross understatement
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep presidential understatement. The casual 'fucking not good' is the perfect tonal note — the profanity signals this is real concern, the understatement signals it's also a political problem. Character-specific to how Selina processes bad news.

jokes.text: We pray for the families. Yeah, and that they don't demand more gun control.
jokes.setup: The standard political response to a shooting is to 'pray for the families'
jokes.punchline: The cynical addendum: they're also praying the families don't politically inconvenience them with gun control demands
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire with a tight structure — the religious formula immediately undercut by naked political self-interest. A bit familiar as a joke type but executed cleanly.

jokes.text: It says here the gunman was an ex-Marine. Two sides of the coin. It's very sad.
jokes.setup: The detail about the gunman being an ex-Marine creates a political complexity (veteran vs. killer)
jokes.punchline: 'Two sides of the coin. Very sad.' — the political calculation delivered in the cadence of genuine sympathy
jokes.explanation: Solid satirical observation about how political teams reduce tragedies to message management. The tonal mimicry of sympathy is the joke.

jokes.text: He's got a thoughts and prayers template.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Mike to draft a statement after the shooting
jokes.punchline: Mike has a pre-made 'thoughts and prayers template' — institutionalizing hollow sympathy
jokes.explanation: The 'thoughts and prayers template' is a sharp satirical concept — the bureaucratization of empathy. Economy is excellent. Has a slight advantage in quotability as a concept that lands beyond the episode.

jokes.text: Should we keep watching me? Yeah? Sure, sure.
jokes.setup: After the shooting news, Selina's team is watching something on TV
jokes.punchline: The revelation that they've been watching coverage of Selina herself — she asks if they should continue watching herself on TV, presented as a real question
jokes.explanation: Excellent character beat — Selina's narcissism is so normalized that watching herself on TV mid-crisis is unremarkable to anyone in the room. The brief self-consultation ('yeah? sure, sure') is perfect.

jokes.text: If I keep going at this rate, I'm gonna have gout of the mouth.
jokes.setup: Dan is complaining about having too many breakfast/brunch/lunch meetings
jokes.punchline: 'Gout of the mouth' — a new disease coined by analogy with the eating disorder/overindulgence of gout, applied to excessive meeting-talking
jokes.explanation: A genuinely inventive coinage. 'Gout of the mouth' works on multiple levels — gout as a disease of excess, here applied to excessive talking/meeting-eating, while also suggesting that Dan's mouth is diseased by all the networking. Highly quotable and original.

jokes.text: Independent, well-educated young women like you who also happen to be very hot to lure congressmen into the room in a way that is deeply feminist.
jokes.setup: The lobbyist describes the role she wants Amy and another woman to play as 'sale bait'
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'in a way that is deeply feminist' applied to literally using women as sexual lures for congressmen
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical bite on DC lobbying culture and the perversion of feminist language to justify exploitation. The deadpan delivery of 'deeply feminist' following an objectifying description is the joke's engine.

jokes.text: We then introduce our client, who makes a sale in a way that's deeply capitalist.
jokes.setup: Following the 'deeply feminist' description of objectifying women
jokes.punchline: The parallel structure applied to the capitalist transaction — 'deeply capitalist' as if it's equally a value system worth honoring
jokes.explanation: A callback that extends the joke through parallel structure. The symmetry of 'deeply feminist / deeply capitalist' makes both terms sound equally hollow. Slightly weaker than the setup joke because the structure is now expected.

jokes.text: I'm not expected to do anything with anyone, am I?
jokes.setup: Amy has just been told she's 'sale bait' for congressmen
jokes.punchline: Her concern isn't fully articulated — she can't even say what she fears, which is more uncomfortable than if she had
jokes.explanation: The elliptical discomfort is the joke — Amy is too professional to say the word but not too proud to be afraid of the implication. Good cringe beat.

jokes.text: This is lobbying. This is a respectable profession. Do me a favor. Go see Linda over there. She has some slightly tighter blouses that you can choose from.
jokes.setup: The lobbyist has just assured Amy that 'this is a respectable profession'
jokes.punchline: In the same breath she directs Amy to get tighter blouses — the contradiction is immediate and delivered without any sense of irony by the speaker
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition is precisely constructed — 'respectable profession' immediately followed by 'tighter blouses' with no acknowledgment of the contradiction. The oblivious delivery amplifies the cringe.

jokes.text: Why are there so many security and military people on the list at this concrete event? Are we at war with clay?
jokes.setup: Amy notices the guest list at a concrete industry event is heavy with military and security figures
jokes.punchline: 'Are we at war with clay?' — an absurd literalization of the military/concrete connection
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Are we at war with clay?' takes exactly the right angle — the absurdist extension of 'concrete enemy' logic to its building-material cousin. Maximum economy (six words), original, and gets funnier as you consider what a war with clay would entail.

jokes.text: Amy, what does the military buy a lot of? Oh, I know this. It's candy.
jokes.setup: The lobbyist asks rhetorically what the military buys a lot of, expecting the obvious answer 'concrete'
jokes.punchline: Amy confidently answers 'candy' — completely wrong, delivered with the certainty of someone who believes they're right
jokes.explanation: A clever character beat — Amy, the sharp political operative, confidently gives the most wrong possible answer. The confidence is what makes it land. Also subverts Amy's typical role as the competent one.

jokes.text: So we bring the military guys along, they talk Congress into stumping up money for a Mexican border wall made of concrete. Circle of life.
jokes.setup: The lobbyist explains the lobbying chain from concrete to border wall
jokes.punchline: Calling the cynical corruption of congressional appropriations 'Circle of Life' — the Disney phrase applied to graft
jokes.explanation: The pop culture reference works here because it's genuinely ironic — the beautiful natural process invoked for the ugliest possible political transaction. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: It's so slutty, isn't it? This place is like a porn shoot with bunting.
jokes.setup: Dan is marveling at how flagrantly transactional the lobbying event is
jokes.punchline: 'A porn shoot with bunting' — the precise image of patriotic decorations draped over naked corruption
jokes.explanation: Outstanding line. 'A porn shoot with bunting' is a genuinely original image — five words that perfectly capture DC lobbying culture, where patriotic trappings dress up transactions that would be illegal in any other context. Highly quotable, memorable, and demonstrates exactly the Veep-style precision simile at its best. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor examples.

jokes.text: The Mommy Meyer Bill. Is it doomed? First off, it's not the Mommy Meyer Bill. It is the Families First... No, it's the Meyer Bill. And it's about as doomed as my donut habit.
jokes.setup: A journalist asks if the Mommy Meyer Bill is doomed — Mike immediately corrects the name but then has to re-correct himself mid-sentence
jokes.punchline: His verbal stumble (Families First... No, the Meyer Bill) exposes the internal chaos, then 'as doomed as my donut habit' is an admission he's fighting a losing battle on both fronts
jokes.explanation: Good Mike character comedy. The self-correction mid-correction is perfectly in character, and 'doomed as my donut habit' works as both denial and admission. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I'm afraid it's getting crucified on the Hill. Just like that Jesus guy.
jokes.setup: Ben says the bill is 'getting crucified on the Hill'
jokes.punchline: 'Just like that Jesus guy' — the casual, almost apologetic clarification of the metaphor, as if uncertain anyone would catch the reference
jokes.explanation: A beautifully timed deadpan. The over-explanation of a well-known metaphor ('just like that Jesus guy') signals either breathtaking casualness or contemptuous condescension. Ben's character — world-weary and contemptuous — makes either reading work. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You think I'm just gonna say it doesn't fit and put it up on craigslist or something?
jokes.setup: Selina is being told her big legislative bill is failing
jokes.punchline: The comparison of a major piece of legislation to an item being listed on Craigslist when it doesn't fit — the mundanity of the comparison is the joke
jokes.explanation: A good Veep-style bathos — the contrast between the gravitas of legislation and the mundanity of Craigslist. Slightly undercut by the trailing 'or something' which weakens the punchline.

jokes.text: O'Brien just said, 'If Families First is passed, the whole concept of family will come to an end.' Well, that's the dream, certainly, but I'm not sure we'll achieve it in this generation.
jokes.setup: O'Brien makes the hyperbolic conservative claim that the bill will end the family
jokes.punchline: Selina plays along as if abolishing the family IS the goal — just an aspirational one that may not be achievable yet
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire. Selina's response accepts O'Brien's framing at face value and treats 'ending the family' as a desirable but ambitious goal — the dark irony working on multiple levels (her own family problems, Democratic policy caricature). Well-structured.

jokes.text: Ma'am, there's an intruder. We need you to remain here. What? I mean, okay. I mean, what? I had not anticipated this. This I had not anticipated. Well, that sounds like the world's worst Dr. Seuss book.
jokes.setup: A genuine security emergency interrupts a meeting — Selina's response is incoherent panic
jokes.punchline: Ben's interjection — 'that sounds like the world's worst Dr. Seuss book' — reframes Selina's panicked repetition as literary nonsense poetry
jokes.explanation: Strong multi-part joke. Selina's panicked non-response is funny on its own (she's useless in a crisis), but Ben's Dr. Seuss observation elevates it — identifying the formal quality of her repetition as accidental absurdist poetry.

jokes.text: Shouldn't we be on an elevator to a secret bunker by now? There is no elevator or bunker. Why isn't there an elevator to a secret bunker?
jokes.setup: During a lockdown, Gary expects the full movie-style presidential security protocol
jokes.punchline: There is no bunker — and Gary's follow-up (why isn't there?) is more alarmed by the infrastructure gap than the actual intruder
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Gary's cinematic expectations versus the mundane reality of actual security. The repeated 'why isn't there' question during a crisis is a nice detail about misplaced priorities.

jokes.text: Jesus Christ, Bill, you're shaking like a dog shitting a peach pit.
jokes.setup: Bill is visibly terrified during the security lockdown
jokes.punchline: The simile 'shaking like a dog shitting a peach pit' — anatomically specific, viscerally funny, and completely unexpected
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. The specificity of 'peach pit' (not just 'bone' — the painful, jagged wrongness of a peach pit) makes this image uniquely vivid and grotesque. It's physically immediate, maximally unexpected, and perfectly captures the kind of coarse wit Ben deploys. Highly quotable and a genuine series-quality line.

jokes.text: I actually think I should have a gun. I'm not making a joke. I really would like to have a gun.
jokes.setup: Bill is terrified during the lockdown
jokes.punchline: His earnest request for a gun, followed by the disclaimer that he's not joking — the disclaimer itself confirming that it sounds exactly like a joke
jokes.explanation: The 'I'm not making a joke' protest works beautifully here — precisely because it sounds like he is. The meta-commentary on his own statement is an unexpected layer of self-awareness from an otherwise panicked character.

jokes.text: Gary, every room you're in is a panic room.
jokes.setup: Gary asks if there's a panic room they can go to
jokes.punchline: Selina reframes the request — Gary's permanent state of anxiety means he's always in a panic room
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Perfect structure — Gary's straight-man question serves as an unintentional setup, and Selina's punchline lands through genuine wordplay (panic room / panic as state of being) while being devastatingly character-specific to Gary's chronic anxiety. Could only be said about Gary, could only be said by Selina. Maximum economy. Ranks among the sharpest lines in the episode.

jokes.text: What are you going to do with a clock? Tell him he's late?
jokes.setup: Someone (Gary) has grabbed a clock as a weapon during the intruder crisis
jokes.punchline: 'Tell him he's late?' — the clock-as-weapon is mocked by pointing out the only thing a clock communicates
jokes.explanation: Clean, well-structured quip. The logical extension (what does a clock communicate?) lands efficiently. Selina's cool contempt for Gary's panic makes it character-appropriate.

jokes.text: He comes here to kill me and he doesn't even know my name?
jokes.setup: The intruder shouted that he wanted to kill 'Tom James and that bitch' — not naming Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's primary grievance about an assassination attempt is the anonymity — she's offended not to have been named
jokes.explanation: The single funniest moment of the episode and one of the best character lines in the series. The joke reveals Selina's pathological need for recognition so completely that survival instinct is secondary to ego. It's also perfectly structured — the setup (she was called 'that bitch') lands, then the punchline exposes the narcissism beneath. Could only be Selina. Maximum economy. Approaches the 9.5 anchor example in character-specific perfection.

jokes.text: He may have been talking about Kent.
jokes.setup: Selina is upset that the intruder called her 'that bitch'
jokes.punchline: Kent calmly suggests the 'bitch' might refer to him — delivered with the same analytical detachment he applies to everything
jokes.explanation: A perfect topper to joke 39. Kent's deadpan self-identification as 'that bitch' — delivered without any wounded pride, just empirical possibility — is both surprising and completely in character. The economy is exceptional (six words) and it gets an additional laugh by extending Selina's joke rather than killing it.

jokes.text: I've got to take a gigantic whiz. Man, the siege stuff goes straight to my bladder.
jokes.setup: The crisis has just been resolved; everyone is processing the adrenaline
jokes.punchline: Ben's immediate post-crisis need is urination, and he explains the physiological mechanism with the matter-of-fact authority he usually reserves for political strategy
jokes.explanation: Ben's gross physicality is a running character trait and this is a solid deployment. 'Siege stuff goes straight to my bladder' has the confident tone of discovered wisdom. Functional.

jokes.text: It's like if dogs could talk.
jokes.setup: Ben has just announced his bladder urgency with great detail
jokes.punchline: He reflects on his own oversharing with 'it's like if dogs could talk' — the comparison is strange, implying that if dogs could articulate their bodily needs they'd talk exactly like Ben
jokes.explanation: A slightly opaque but genuinely funny non-sequitur. The image of talking dogs discussing their bodily functions with Ben's authority is absurd and apt. It works better on repeat viewing when you understand Ben's character.

jokes.text: I saw him! The guy. I was face-to-face with him and I was like, 'Back off, man. Don't mess with this.' I have got so much adrenaline in me. Can you get me a coffee? An Irish coffee.
jokes.setup: Gary is claiming heroic confrontation with the intruder
jokes.punchline: The 'badass' monologue immediately collapses into asking for an Irish coffee — the request for liquid courage retroactively deflates all the bravado
jokes.explanation: Well-structured escalation/deflation. Gary's braggadocio is immediately punctured by his request for an Irish coffee — the alcohol tells the real story. Character-specific and earned.

jokes.text: That's exactly how it was, dumbass.
jokes.setup: A TV producer/director type has been micromanaging lighting adjustments, telling a technician to bring lights up and down until they arrive back at the original setting
jokes.punchline: The technician's flat 'that's exactly how it was, dumbass' — unexpectedly direct and profane from someone presumably in a subservient role
jokes.explanation: A clean reaction beat. The authority figure being dressed down by the worker they're condescending to is a reliable source of comedy, and 'dumbass' is perfectly timed after all the fussy adjustments.

jokes.text: Hit me with the plosives. Bring back my pig.
jokes.setup: A TV personality is doing sound checks
jokes.punchline: 'Bring back my pig' — an apparently sincere request that makes no contextual sense, delivered with the same authority as the technical direction
jokes.explanation: A tiny absurdist gem. 'Bring back my pig' delivered with total authority during a technical briefing is a pure non-sequitur that rewards the attentive viewer. The context gap is the joke.

jokes.text: Everybody thinks we're awesome here. I mean, we're West Wing. We're like gods.
jokes.setup: Characters are on what appears to be a campaign stop or event, trying to boost morale.
jokes.punchline: Calling themselves 'West Wing' and 'gods' when everything we've seen in the episode suggests they're barely functional is a sustained irony.
jokes.explanation: Functional irony given the context — the show has been established as the un-West Wing — but the joke doesn't have a sharp punchline of its own. It relies on the audience's meta-awareness of the show's premise. Competent.

jokes.text: Yeah, help you forget about being molested. That's loud. I'm sorry.
jokes.setup: Someone is saying the campaign trail will help them forget about something difficult (presumably the intruder incident or political pressure)
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response — 'help you forget about being molested' — is so inappropriately dark that even Jonah immediately apologizes for the volume rather than the content
jokes.explanation: Classic Jonah — he says the worst possible thing with zero social calibration, then corrects the wrong part of it ('that's loud, I'm sorry' rather than 'that was inappropriate'). Character-consistent and dark.

jokes.text: Mr. Ryan, did you get my sashimi? No, sir, I will get it right now. It needs to be at room temperature, otherwise it just tastes of cold.
jokes.setup: Jonah asserts his authority over his assistant Richard by sending him for sashimi
jokes.punchline: 'Otherwise it just tastes of cold' — an absurd tautological food complaint that treats 'cold' as a flavor
jokes.explanation: 'Tastes of cold' is a genuinely funny piece of non-logic delivered as expertise. Jonah's performative food snobbery in a political context — and the specific absurdity of room-temperature sashimi — is strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Sa-shi-mi. Listen to the shus and the mis. Are you hearing this? Sushi.
jokes.setup: Jonah is correcting someone's pronunciation of 'sashimi'
jokes.punchline: His mnemonic ('listen to the shus and the mis') is as wrong and condescending as possible — 'sashimi' has no 'shus' — and he then seems to confuse it with 'sushi'
jokes.explanation: Jonah's Dunning-Kruger at its finest — he's confidently wrong about pronunciation while condescending to others. The confusion with 'sushi' at the end may be from someone else catching the error, which amplifies the joke.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. What are we laughing at here? I don't get it. Oh, duck 'cause I ducked. Very clever. Very funny. I'm laughing, too.
jokes.setup: The room is laughing at something (presumably a visual joke or a moment Jonah missed)
jokes.punchline: Jonah's delayed comprehension, followed by his hollow 'I'm laughing, too' — performative laughter that exposes he didn't get it
jokes.explanation: Jonah's social incompetence on display — he misses a joke, then desperately performs getting it. 'I'm laughing, too' is the perfect flat cap to the sequence. Character-specific cringe.

jokes.text: Mom, some days you get an award from a country I've never even heard of, then other days people try to kill you.
jokes.setup: Catherine is processing the assassination attempt on her mother
jokes.punchline: The bathetic equivalence of 'award from unknown country' and 'people try to kill you' as equivalent life events — plus the jab at the obscurity of the award
jokes.explanation: Catherine's outsider perspective on presidential life produces a sharp observation. The casual equivalence between award and assassination attempt, and the dismissive 'country I've never heard of,' both land well.

jokes.text: People probably try to kill me most days, sweetie. You just have to shut it out.
jokes.setup: Catherine expresses horror at the assassination attempt
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual correction — it happens most days, you just tune it out — delivered as parenting advice
jokes.explanation: Strong deadpan. 'You just have to shut it out' applied to assassination attempts, delivered as maternal wisdom, captures Selina's compartmentalization and the show's dark worldview efficiently.

jokes.text: I do love tasting menus. It's so nice to be able to look at a list of food and say, 'I'll take all of it.'
jokes.setup: The chef has brought a tasting menu for Selina's dinner
jokes.punchline: Selina reframes the tasting menu's purpose — instead of curated small portions, she sees it as the option to have everything simultaneously
jokes.explanation: A perceptive character joke — Selina's appetite for everything, framed as the privilege of power. The tasting menu logic inversion is elegant.

jokes.text: Okay, you know I almost got assassinated today, right? Yeah, you did. So I just need you to do what I'm telling you to do.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to get her chef to simply serve pizza instead of a deconstructed version
jokes.punchline: She invokes the assassination attempt as leverage for getting a simple pizza — the gravitas of nearly dying weaponized against culinary pretension
jokes.explanation: The assassination attempt as social currency — Selina cashing it in not for anything meaningful but for a less fancy pizza. The bathos of the exchange is perfect Veep.

jokes.text: So of course he would like to win the new one, which means he needs to speak to Congressman Martin, but not his obvious erection.
jokes.setup: The lobbyist is explaining that a congressman is very excited about meeting the concrete contractor
jokes.punchline: The word 'erection' used for a building/construction context but landing as an anatomical observation about the congressman — and she's clearly talking about both
jokes.explanation: A clean double entendre with construction-industry context. The lobbyist's breezy deployment of 'obvious erection' — as if it's merely inconvenient rather than scandalous — is the tone that makes it work.

jokes.text: Ah, to be 20 years younger, right? I would be in diapers.
jokes.setup: The older contractor makes a lecherous comment about wishing he were younger
jokes.punchline: Amy's comeback — 'I would be in diapers' — is an age-gap shutdown delivered with the confidence of someone who knows she's won the exchange
jokes.explanation: A sharp, well-timed retort. Amy's 'I would be in diapers' is maximally economical and does the work of exposing the creepiness of the situation without belaboring it. The 'Oh, snap' from the lobbyist confirms the room felt it.

jokes.text: He's nailing it, like Streisand hitting a high C. My God, where were you born, Lake Homo?
jokes.setup: Tom James is giving an apparently compelling speech about Families First
jokes.punchline: Ben's admiration is expressed first through a Streisand simile (which itself signals something), then immediately followed by 'Lake Homo' — a homophobic joke that is simultaneously self-incriminating given the reference choices
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels — Ben chooses Streisand as his gold standard, then mocks himself for it. The internal contradiction (Streisand reference / homophobic joke) is the real gag, suggesting Ben's self-awareness is not quite there.

jokes.text: Reaction is easy. Understanding is hard. Wasn't the shooter also a victim?
jokes.setup: Tom James is asked about gun control following a mass shooting
jokes.punchline: His philosophical pivot — 'wasn't the shooter also a victim?' — delivered with senatorial gravitas, immediately creating a political disaster
jokes.explanation: The cringe is in the genuine political stupidity of someone who thinks he's being profound. 'Reaction is easy, understanding is hard' is exactly the kind of meaningless-deep political aphorism that sounds good until applied to an active shooter. The political disaster it creates is the punchline.

jokes.text: Calamity James. And that's just off the top of my head.
jokes.setup: Ben hears that Tom James has publicly sympathized with the Pittsburgh shooter
jokes.punchline: 'Calamity James' — a new nickname coinage, immediately self-satisfied with the qualifier 'off the top of my head'
jokes.explanation: 'Calamity James' is a crisp political nickname that works on its own, but 'and that's just off the top of my head' transforms it into a character joke — Ben's casual genius for political destruction. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Just get him off, you fucking mannequin.
jokes.setup: Ben is watching in horror as Tom James keeps talking at the podium, digging himself deeper
jokes.punchline: The insult 'fucking mannequin' directed at whoever is supposed to be managing James — perfectly calibrated contempt
jokes.explanation: 'Fucking mannequin' is an original and vivid insult — it implies stillness, emptiness, decorative uselessness. Economy is near-perfect. Ben's casual brutality at peak stress is a series highlight.

jokes.text: It looks like you're telling him to straighten his tie. What the fuck are you doing?
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to signal to Tom James to stop talking, apparently through some kind of hand gesture
jokes.punchline: Ben's observation that the frantic 'stop talking' signal looks like a tie-straightening gesture — Jonah's incompetence expressed physically
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy inferred from dialogue. Jonah's ineffectual attempt to stop a political disaster, misread as a fashion cue, captures his fundamental uselessness in a crisis. Physical comedy rendered through observation.

jokes.text: Maybe we could play some exit music or maybe push the button that drops the balloons. Shit, that's a great idea. Drop the balloons. Do we have balloons? No. That was just an example. Why the fuck did you bring up balloons if we don't have them?
jokes.setup: The team needs to get Tom James off the podium before he causes more damage
jokes.punchline: A brainstorming session that immediately commits to balloons, discovers there are no balloons, and then blames the person who suggested balloons as an example
jokes.explanation: A beautifully escalating comedic structure. The balloon bit has three beats: suggestion, commitment, retraction, blame — each funnier than the last. 'Why the fuck did you bring up balloons if we don't have them?' is a perfect expression of political crisis mismanagement.

jokes.text: I thought I would say, 'So I'm in the firing line for the second time today.' Hilarious, ma'am. Yeah, I know, it's not that funny. No, it's just my voice. I always end up sounding sarcastic.
jokes.setup: Selina has prepared an opening joke about being in the 'firing line'
jokes.punchline: Mike says 'hilarious' so flatly that it sounds sarcastic — then blames his voice for always sounding sarcastic, which is itself sarcastic
jokes.explanation: A meta-joke about sycophancy breaking down under pressure. Mike's 'hilarious' is so obviously flat that he has to qualify it, and the qualification ('I always sound sarcastic') is itself a perfect self-indictment.

jokes.text: I always feel as if I'm in more danger when I'm in front of you guys.
jokes.setup: Selina is addressing the press having just survived a real security threat
jokes.punchline: She implies the press are more dangerous than the actual intruder — delivered as flattery's dark inverse
jokes.explanation: A competent press joke — politicians saying 'the press is tougher than my enemies' is well-worn territory. Functional given context but not original.

jokes.text: This is the second time I'm in front of the firing squad today.
jokes.setup: Selina uses the 'firing line' joke she had prepared earlier
jokes.punchline: She botches it — 'firing squad' instead of 'firing line,' which implies she's about to be executed rather than just scrutinized
jokes.explanation: A tight callback that pays off joke 62 — the joke Selina rehearsed gets garbled in delivery ('firing squad' vs. 'firing line'), which is simultaneously worse AND funnier than what she planned.

jokes.text: Mike, I'm good. I'm gonna duck out for now. You got me. Yeah, I did. So you can take over, you bozo.
jokes.setup: Selina has just barely survived the press conference after the Tom James disaster
jokes.punchline: 'You can take over, you bozo' — the handoff delivered as insult, leaving Mike alone to face the press wolves after she escapes
jokes.explanation: Selina's casual cruelty in abandoning Mike to the press is pure character. 'You bozo' as the parting word while leaving someone to manage a crisis is perfectly Selina.

jokes.text: We have a good time here, don't we?
jokes.setup: Mike has just been abandoned by Selina to face the press alone after a political disaster
jokes.punchline: His rhetorical question to the press — 'we have a good time here, don't we?' — is a desperate stall tactic that inadvertently exposes how bad the situation is
jokes.explanation: Mike's haplessness crystallized in a single question. The detachment of 'we have a good time here, don't we?' from the actual situation is perfect spokesman-in-over-his-head comedy.

jokes.text: That is obviously... What is clear... That our view... Is we don't support that view.
jokes.setup: Mike is asked point-blank whether the administration supports Tom James's claim that the shooter was a victim
jokes.punchline: Mike's attempt at a simple answer falls apart in real time — three false starts, each more committed than the last, before landing on the tautological 'we don't support that view'
jokes.explanation: Mike's public disintegration is the episode's sustained cringe peak. The false starts building to 'we don't support that view' — which says almost nothing — is a perfect depiction of a press secretary unable to think on his feet. Well-paced escalation.

jokes.text: Words don't kill people, unlike guns, which can and did. Which is bad. It's so bad. I could go on.
jokes.setup: Mike tries to navigate gun control vs. Tom James's words causing the political damage
jokes.punchline: His attempted NRA-style framing ('words don't kill people') immediately collapses when he has to acknowledge guns do, and then he can't stop qualifying it ('which is bad. It's so bad. I could go on.')
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout moments. 'Words don't kill people, unlike guns, which can and did. Which is bad. It's so bad. I could go on.' is a masterpiece of incremental cringe. Each phrase is worse than the last — the NRA framing abandoned, replaced by escalating qualifications, culminating in 'I could go on' which implies there's more to say but also that he's torturing himself. Highly quotable and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Tom James just sympathized with that Pittsburgh shooter and in doing so has blown his political brains out.
jokes.setup: The team is assessing the political damage from Tom James's statement
jokes.punchline: 'Blown his political brains out' — a gunshot metaphor applied to political self-destruction, in the context of a gun violence story
jokes.explanation: The gun metaphor applied to political suicide in the context of a gun story is a nice dark irony, but it's a fairly standard political idiom pushed just one degree darker.

jokes.text: He just made an enemy of every stupid person with a gun, and there are a lot of those.
jokes.setup: Tom James has sympathized with a shooter, alienating gun rights supporters
jokes.punchline: 'Every stupid person with a gun, and there are a lot of those' — the qualifier reduces gun rights voters to a caricature with cheerful specificity
jokes.explanation: The parenthetical 'and there are a lot of those' is doing the heavy lifting — the matter-of-fact acknowledgment of the constituency's size amplifies the contempt. Clean and quotable.

jokes.text: If we don't do something about this, our gun and security clients are gonna start to wonder why it is the hell they pay us so much money, which is bad because I need them to pay me and not try and shoot me in my sleep.
jokes.setup: They need to manage the political fallout to protect their lobbying clients' interests
jokes.punchline: The pivot from professional concern to personal fear of being shot by their own gun clients — the industry's violence coming back around to the lobbyists
jokes.explanation: The dark irony of lobbyists fearing their own clients' product is sharp satirical logic. Slightly overlong but the core insight lands.

jokes.text: Very well done. You might be the smartest woman I've ever paid for.
jokes.setup: Amy has just solved the strategic problem efficiently
jokes.punchline: 'The smartest woman I've ever paid for' — the compliment is simultaneously genuine and reductive, treating Amy as a commodity
jokes.explanation: A precise cringe compliment — the qualifier 'I've ever paid for' transforms the praise into objectification without the speaker noticing. Economical and sharp, with layers of meaning about how women are valued in this world.

jokes.text: Oh, man, I'm really enjoying this sibling rivalry between you two. I'm trying to figure out what that makes you to us. Daddy. Ah.
jokes.setup: The boss observes the competitive dynamic between Dan and Amy
jokes.punchline: 'Daddy' — whoever answers (Dan or Amy) reframes the power dynamic as a family one, which the boss accepts with a satisfied 'Ah'
jokes.explanation: The quick 'daddy' answer with the beat of recognition is a sharp exchange. The sibling rivalry/parental dynamic captures the lobbying world's infantilizing structure.

jokes.text: I am not Merlin, okay? I don't have a magic car or whatever he had.
jokes.setup: Mike is being pressured to fix the Tom James disaster through PR magic
jokes.punchline: Mike's Merlin reference is already confused (Merlin had a wand, not a car), and the qualifier 'or whatever he had' signals he knows he's wrong but doesn't care enough to correct himself
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed Mike moment. The Merlin reference is already wrong (magic car?), but 'or whatever he had' is the killer — it shows Mike knows his analogy is broken but presses forward anyway. This is Mike's incompetence made linguistic.

jokes.text: You'll tie a ribbon around Elvis's sagging, rotten ball sack if we ask you to, Mike. That's your job.
jokes.setup: Mike is pushing back on being asked to manage Tom James's disaster
jokes.punchline: Selina's image of what Mike's job actually is — tying a ribbon around a decomposing Elvis — is grotesquely specific and maximally degrading
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The image is perfectly constructed: Elvis (American icon, died badly), sagging and rotten (specific, grotesque), ball sack (anatomically degrading), ribbon (cheerful domesticity). The contrast between the decorative 'ribbon' and the horror beneath it perfectly encapsulates what a press secretary does. Highly quotable and sharply original.

jokes.text: Tom James took a crap all over America. Why do I got to grab the shovel? I'm not a shit cleaner. It's your job. You are the shit shoveler. Well, some shit doesn't get off. What? I don't know what that means.
jokes.setup: Mike is protesting his role in cleaning up the Tom James disaster
jokes.punchline: The extended scatological metaphor collapses when Mike says 'some shit doesn't get off' — a phrase that makes no sense — and even Mike admits he doesn't know what it means
jokes.explanation: The scatological argument is funny throughout, but the punchline — Mike deploying a meaningless phrase and then acknowledging its meaninglessness — is the real joke. It's a perfect portrait of someone who talks his way into corners.

jokes.text: Kill the Mommy, okay? 'Cause it's making my bill toxic. It's like they took the word brain tumor and changed it to Selina Cyst.
jokes.setup: Selina wants the 'Mommy' dropped from 'Mommy Meyer Bill'
jokes.punchline: The simile 'brain tumor changed to Selina Cyst' — the Mommy prefix makes the bill seem both personal and malignant, like a benign title acquiring medical horror
jokes.explanation: Outstanding simile. 'Brain tumor changed to Selina Cyst' is layered — a brain tumor is at least dramatic and serious, a cyst is diminutive and gross. The personalization ('Selina Cyst') makes it worse by attaching her name to something medically unpleasant. Both readings (tumor vs. cyst, and Selina's name becoming pathological) work simultaneously. A top-tier Veep image.

jokes.text: He's going freestyle. God, that is so fucking gangster.
jokes.setup: Tom James has abandoned his prepared statement to speak from the heart about the Pittsburgh shooting
jokes.punchline: Jonah's street-slang response ('so fucking gangster') to what is in fact a sincere political apology — the generational/cultural mismatch is the joke
jokes.explanation: Jonah using 'gangster' in a political context is a consistent character beat — his social-media brain translates everything into performative coolness. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, I can't say that that felt good, because obviously it felt terrible, but it also felt good.
jokes.setup: Tom James has just given a heartfelt public apology for his mistake
jokes.punchline: His post-apology self-assessment cancels itself out in real time — 'felt terrible but also felt good' — the politician unable to resist self-congratulation even while acknowledging failure
jokes.explanation: Tom James's self-awareness and ego cancel each other out beautifully. The syntax mirrors his mental process — 'I can't say it felt good... obviously terrible... but also good.' A good character reveal.

jokes.text: Sir, awesome and amazing had a baby and it grew up to be you.
jokes.setup: Richard is complimenting Tom James on his apology
jokes.punchline: 'Awesome and amazing had a baby and it grew up to be you' — an extended metaphorical compliment that is both absurdly wholesome and endearingly sincere
jokes.explanation: Richard's character — earnest, literal, operating without irony — makes this line work. His compliments always extend one beat too far. This is perfectly Richard: the sentiment is real, the execution is uniquely strange.

jokes.text: It's like words are your second language, sir.
jokes.setup: Richard is on a roll complimenting Tom James
jokes.punchline: 'Words are your second language' — intended as a compliment about eloquence but technically meaning Tom James struggles with words
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a series-quality Richard moment. 'Words are your second language' is intended as the highest praise — but technically says Tom James is not a native speaker of words, which undermines everything. Richard delivers this with complete sincerity, making the double meaning invisible to him but crystalline to the audience. Maximum character integration, excellent economy.

jokes.text: Richard T. Splett. Don't know why I said T. My middle name is John.
jokes.setup: Richard introduces himself to Tom James, unnecessarily adding his middle initial.
jokes.punchline: Richard immediately undermines his own introduction by confessing he doesn't know why he used 'T' when his middle name is John — making the whole formality pointless.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Richard Splett joke. The immediate self-undermining ('Don't know why I said T') followed by the confession that his middle name is John — meaning neither 'T' nor anything is accurate — is exactly how Richard operates. He overthinks, over-performs, and then immediately cancels himself. Near-perfect character integration. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan. That's a name that keeps popping up. Well, I'm a pop-ular guy. Richard T. Splett. Don't know why I said T. My middle name is John.
jokes.setup: Tom James meets Jonah and Richard
jokes.punchline: Two-part: Jonah's 'popular' pun off 'popping up,' followed immediately by Richard introducing himself with a middle initial he then can't explain
jokes.explanation: Richard T. Splett is a rich character detail — the inexplicable middle initial followed by the immediate correction is perfectly in character for Richard's earnest social misfiring. Jonah's pun is weak but Richard's beat is strong.

jokes.text: So you're this guy's veep? Kind of. Kind of, yeah.
jokes.setup: Tom James observes that Richard is Jonah's 'veep' (executive assistant)
jokes.punchline: The parallel to Selina's situation as 'veep' — Jonah has his own subordinate, just as Selina has her own, but 'kind of, kind of, yeah' suggests Richard isn't sure this is accurate
jokes.explanation: A structural joke about power hierarchies — Jonah has recreated the show's own power structure in miniature. Richard's 'kind of' acknowledges the humiliation while the show acknowledges the irony.

jokes.text: Hey, we should have you apologize for all our screwups. Hurricane washout. Leon West 'detention.' The data breach. Oh, yeah, that thing with the HIV girl. Yes. Wow, that HIV girl.
jokes.setup: The team is impressed by Tom James's apology
jokes.punchline: The laundry list of scandals to be apologized for escalates in darkness — ending with 'wow, that HIV girl' as a shared grimace
jokes.explanation: The catalogue of disasters functions as dark comedy world-building. 'Wow, that HIV girl' landing last as the worst thing — with mutual recognition — is a good escalation cap.

jokes.text: Wait a sec... If I didn't know better, I would suspect that there was another data breach. Really? I mean, there isn't. No, because I would know about it. Mm.
jokes.setup: Tom James probes after the HIV girl mention
jokes.punchline: The awkward confirmations — 'there isn't' / 'I would know about it' / 'Mm' — each less convincing than the last, building to a silent confirmation
jokes.explanation: The diplomatic 'Mm' that ends the exchange is doing the most work — a perfect non-verbal confirmation. The cringe of Jonah and Richard trying to conceal something from someone clearly smarter than them is well-played.

jokes.text: How did that go down? Surprisingly well. Like vodka and cereal.
jokes.setup: Tom James asks how his apology went over
jokes.punchline: 'Like vodka and cereal' — an incongruous food/drink pairing that implies the apology succeeded through sheer wrongness working somehow
jokes.explanation: A superb invented simile in the Veep tradition. 'Vodka and cereal' — not vodka and orange juice (which would imply a mimosa-style success) but vodka and cereal, which works only through desperate necessity and still feels wrong while getting the job done. Highly quotable and original.

jokes.text: Gun guys don't like it when you take things away from them.
jokes.setup: They're deciding whether to drop the gun-industry client after Tom James's apology made the relationship complicated
jokes.punchline: The literal truth that gun people resist confiscation, applied as lobbying strategy
jokes.explanation: A competent political joke using the obvious double meaning (gun guys / taking things away). Functional but not exceptional — this is well-trodden territory.

jokes.text: Well, construction brings builders. Builders bring sex workers. And sex workers bring STDs. A virtuous circle.
jokes.setup: Amy asks what a pharma rep is doing at a concrete event
jokes.punchline: The pharmaceutical rep explains via a chain of logic: construction → builders → sex workers → STDs → pharma. The chain is delivered with corporate smoothness and capped with 'a virtuous circle'
jokes.explanation: A standout joke and a genuine series-quality moment. The structure is perfect: each link in the chain is plausible and darkly logical, and 'a virtuous circle' applies corporate/economic language to a chain of human exploitation. It manages to satirize the pharmaceutical industry, the construction industry, and the 'circle of life' corporate philosophy simultaneously. Economy is exceptional — four short sentences. This rivals the best Veep jokes.

jokes.text: Oh, you are hotter than ever. What do you use, like rhino horns or monkey glands?
jokes.setup: Selina's old friends reunite and one compliments Selina's appearance
jokes.punchline: 'Rhino horns or monkey glands' — the compliment assumes Selina's good looks must require exotic, possibly illegal, animal-based remedies
jokes.explanation: An unusual and original compliment that loops through exotic black-market remedies. 'Monkey glands' specifically is absurdly specific and aged-sounding, giving it a period character quality.

jokes.text: No, I didn't really have any surgery, Deborah. I didn't. I haven't had... no.
jokes.setup: Selina's friend says she had her whole face done, Selina protests she's all natural
jokes.punchline: Selina's increasingly fragmented denial — 'I didn't. I haven't had... no.' — the ellipsis suggesting she's reconsidering what exactly she has or hasn't had done
jokes.explanation: The trailing '...no' tells the real story — Selina's certainty about her own surgical history is less than certain. Classic cringe denial comedy.

jokes.text: It's booze, you old boozehound.
jokes.setup: The British ambassador's sloe gin is being described in formal terms
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts through the formality with 'it's booze, you old boozehound' — the contrast between diplomatic gift and Selina's characterization
jokes.explanation: The word 'boozehound' as presidential vocabulary is characterful. Functional comic deflation of diplomatic ceremony.

jokes.text: Actually, I have been sober for five years and four months, so... I have been sober for 27 hours, so let me take that off your hand while I jump off the wagon.
jokes.setup: Selina offers the gin to a friend who is revealed to be sober
jokes.punchline: Selina responds by announcing her own sobriety ('27 hours') and immediately jumping off the wagon in front of her friend, treating recovery as a competitive stat
jokes.explanation: Excellent comic structure. The contrast between 'five years and four months' (precise, hard-won) and '27 hours' (barely qualifies as sobriety) is the setup. Selina treating sobriety as a competition she's already losing — and jumping off the wagon in the same breath — is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Benjamin, you wanted to see me? Is this where I get whacked? Oh, no, sir. This is where we all blow you because you were so freaking great.
jokes.setup: Tom James is called to see Ben, anticipating retribution
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response replaces the Mafia threat with a crude sexual compliment — 'blow you' used as enthusiastic praise
jokes.explanation: The Mafia/mob framing followed by Jonah's enthusiastic misuse of language is a solid bit. Jonah's obliviousness to the innuendo is character-consistent.

jokes.text: He's like your dad except he's nice.
jokes.setup: Someone observes Tom James's likability
jokes.punchline: Jonah's compliment casually implies that Jonah's own father is not nice — revealing something about Jonah's psychology while ostensibly complimenting someone else
jokes.explanation: A brilliant five-word character reveal. 'Like your dad except he's nice' assumes the baseline is an unpleasant father — and from Jonah, this lands as dark autobiography. Maximum economy with a psychological payload.

jokes.text: What the fuck are these guys doing here? Every player needs to roll with a crew, Ben. That's the truth from the street.
jokes.setup: Ben is surprised to find Jonah and Richard at the drinks gathering
jokes.punchline: Jonah explaining his entourage using street gang logic — 'truth from the street' — in a White House context
jokes.explanation: Jonah's persistent misapplication of street credibility to political contexts is a consistent character trait. 'Truth from the street' is a good line but slightly over-familiar as a Jonah bit.

jokes.text: This is officially my worst day.
jokes.setup: Ben is surrounded by Jonah and Richard, having just processed a catastrophic political day
jokes.punchline: Ben's declaration — 'officially my worst day' — delivered with complete flatness about a day that included a shooting, an assassination attempt, and a political meltdown
jokes.explanation: Ben's weary collapse into 'worst day' — and that it's the presence of Jonah and Richard, not any of the actual crises, that tips the scale — is a nice character insight.

jokes.text: I like my bourbon like I like my women... 18 years old and wet.
jokes.setup: Friday night drinks have begun; Ben is establishing his preferences
jokes.punchline: The classic 'I like my X like my women' structure redirected to bourbon — the 18-year-old age is genuinely disturbing when applied to women, and 'wet' applies to both
jokes.explanation: A well-worn joke structure ('I like my X like I like my women') deployed here mostly for shock via the '18-year-old' age reference. Scores lower on originality and earned because this joke template is extremely familiar and the dark shock is its only tool. Ben's character makes it fit but doesn't elevate it.

jokes.text: I'm more of a white Russian man myself. Here, take that. Okay, I guess that's fine also. Have a seat. Not there. No. Wasn't going to.
jokes.setup: Richard announces his drink preference (white Russian) and is handed something else
jokes.punchline: Richard's accepting 'I guess that's fine also' and then the seat refusal/acceptance — Ben's territory is established through micro-power moves, and Richard accepts each diminishment
jokes.explanation: Richard's gentle capitulation to every social diminishment is perfect character comedy. 'Wasn't going to' from Jonah is also a nice detail — he hadn't planned to sit in Ben's seat but pretends he had no intention regardless.

jokes.text: Not there. — No. Wasn't going to.
jokes.setup: Ben is telling someone (Jonah) where to sit
jokes.punchline: Whoever is told 'not there' immediately says 'wasn't going to' — they were absolutely going to
jokes.explanation: A quick face-saving bit. The transparent lie 'wasn't going to' after being told 'not there' is a classic social-humiliation beat. Short, functional, works in context.

jokes.text: Anna's wedding. That was 20 years ago. Oh, no, it was five years ago. What? She got married again. Oh. Well, that doesn't surprise me. Yeah. Did he finally come out? He died. Oh. I am so sorry.
jokes.setup: The women are reminiscing about when they were last together at Anna's wedding
jokes.punchline: The conversation assumes the husband 'came out' (as gay) — a reasonable social assumption — but he actually died, making the attempted worldly sophistication of 'did he finally come out?' land with accidental callousness
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. The setup (20 years → no, 5 years → she remarried) misdirects toward a simple timeline joke. Then the pivot to the husband — the audience's expectation matches the character's ('did he come out?') — then death. Three beats, each recontextualizing the last. The cringe of 'I am so sorry' is the perfect cap. One of the episode's most carefully constructed jokes.

jokes.text: I'm not. I mean, he wasn't gay. He was just an asshole. Can I swear in front of you now? No, you fucking can't.
jokes.setup: After Selina apologizes for Anna's husband's death, Anna sets the record straight about his character
jokes.punchline: Two-part: (1) 'He wasn't gay, he was just an asshole' — clearing up the assumption while delivering a blunt epitaph. (2) 'Can I swear in front of you now?' / 'No, you fucking can't' — the response uses profanity to prohibit profanity
jokes.explanation: The topper to joke 99 and a standout in its own right. 'No, you fucking can't' as a response to 'can I swear' is a perfect grammatical self-contradiction — using profanity to refuse permission for profanity. The epitaph 'he wasn't gay, he was just an asshole' is also exactly right — clears the assumption without sentiment. Two big laughs in three sentences.

jokes.text: What's for dessert, a tub of ice cream and four spoons? Yes.
jokes.setup: The pizza dinner is winding down
jokes.punchline: The rhetorical 'a tub of ice cream and four spoons?' gets the sincere answer 'yes' — Selina has actually done exactly this, even though it was offered as an absurd joke
jokes.explanation: A clean comic surprise — the rhetorical question answered literally. The 'yes' is a single word doing enormous work: it confirms the absurd premise, reveals Selina's commitment to the night, and gets a laugh through pure economy.

jokes.text: Any other psychotic things that we need to know about you? I think we should sterilize everyone in New Jersey. Good idea. I'm in favor of assisted dying for anyone who talks in a movie theater. That's forward thinking. And I think we should legalize drugs. Oh, yeah! I'll get the bong.
jokes.setup: The relaxed girls' night is prompting increasingly extreme policy positions as humor
jokes.punchline: The positions escalate in transgression — sterilization, assisted dying, drug legalization — each accepted by the group as a reasonable joke until the last one turns out not to be a joke
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure. The group's casual agreement with increasingly extreme positions — 'Good idea' / 'That's forward thinking' — works as a satire of dinner party politics, building toward the reversal of the drug legalization beat.

jokes.text: No, no, no, that last one, that's actually not a joke.
jokes.setup: The friend has apparently been joking through the extreme positions
jokes.punchline: She clarifies the drugs position is sincere — the reversal of what was expected (the others seemed jokey, the most extreme one is real)
jokes.explanation: The setup-reveal structure is well-executed: sterilization and assisted dying were jokes, drug legalization is not. The subversion of expectation — the one thing that sounds most reasonable politically is the one thing she means — lands well.

jokes.text: I'm serious. I believe we should legalize drugs. Having seen what my son has been through, I think it's the only way. Fuck my face.
jokes.setup: The friend elaborates that her drug legalization position is personal — her son
jokes.punchline: Selina's reaction to the political liability of what her friend just confessed — 'Fuck my face' — the presidential pivot from empathy to panic
jokes.explanation: 'Fuck my face' is a Veep-grade reaction. The phrase itself is grotesquely original — not 'fuck my life' but 'face,' specifically, as if even her face is implicated in the political disaster. It captures Selina's instantaneous pivot from empathy to self-preservation. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Okay, if this gets out, my life's hell. Guys, guys! Come on. Everybody calm down. Yeah, let's just smoke a doobie and pop some ludes.
jokes.setup: Selina and her team are panicking about the drug confession becoming public
jokes.punchline: Someone's response to the drug policy crisis is to suggest smoking and taking Quaaludes — applying the drug solution to the drug problem
jokes.explanation: A functional quip that applies the obvious irony (the solution to the drug problem is drugs). 'Ludes' is dated slang that gives it a slightly anachronistic humor.

jokes.text: Look at us, we're working it from both ends. Um, well, because I'm about to pass this legislation.
jokes.setup: Deborah does pro bono family law work; Selina is passing Families First
jokes.punchline: Selina's claim that they're 'working it from both ends' — which she means sincerely about policy but which sounds uncomfortable — and her explanation is a political non-answer
jokes.explanation: A mild double entendre that the show doesn't fully commit to. The self-consciousness in the moment undercuts the punchline. Functional but weak.

jokes.text: Uh, perhaps you haven't thought through the tax margins properly. We have the finest economic minds working on the bill. Thank God, because tax law was never your strength. I really read up about it a lot. Oh, my God. Do you remember? What's 15%? What's 15%?
jokes.setup: Selina's old colleagues are scrutinizing her flagship bill
jokes.punchline: The humiliation of Selina's professional failures being trotted out at her own dinner party by friends who knew her when — 'tax law was never your strength' / 'What's 15%?' as the embarrassing callback
jokes.explanation: The cringe of old friends exposing current incompetence is a strong comic situation. 'What's 15%?' as the ancient embarrassing quiz question is a nice specific detail — not a general 'you were bad at math' but a specific remembered humiliation.

jokes.text: What's 15%? What's 15%? / I don't want to talk about politics anymore.
jokes.setup: A friend is apparently recounting a moment when Selina couldn't calculate a basic percentage — presumably from their law days together.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I don't want to talk about politics anymore' — retreating from a mathematical embarrassment by suddenly claiming it's a political topic, to shut down the anecdote.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from mathematical embarrassment to 'I don't want to talk about politics' is a perfect Selina maneuver — reframing a personal competency failure as a political discussion she's choosing to end. The speed and decisiveness of the deflection, and the friends' immediate acceptance, is excellent. High character integration.

jokes.text: She can't have wine.
jokes.setup: The dinner is getting uncomfortable and someone is presumably offering wine around
jokes.punchline: Selina's sotto voce 'she can't have wine' — presumably about her sober friend — delivered as logistical information while the social situation crumbles
jokes.explanation: A small functional beat. The quiet practicality of 'she can't have wine' amid the larger social crisis has a nice deadpan quality.

jokes.text: Jeez, I wouldn't last 10 seconds in a men's room. But between us, I just think honesty is the best policy. So, in that spirit, maybe somebody could now kindly tell me what the fuck is this other data breach.
jokes.setup: Tom James is presenting himself as honest and direct
jokes.punchline: His entire 'honesty is the best policy' setup is revealed to be a manipulation tactic to extract information — and then 'what the fuck is this other data breach' as the demand
jokes.explanation: Tom James's political manipulation is perfectly captured — the elaborate setup of 'honesty is the best policy' immediately weaponized. 'What the fuck is this other data breach' as the punchline after all the diplomatic framing is well-structured misdirection.

jokes.text: Do you recognize her? She lost all of her baby fat.
jokes.setup: Catherine appears at the dinner unexpectedly.
jokes.punchline: Selina introduces her own daughter to her friends by noting that Catherine 'lost all of her baby fat' — an introduction that's both a compliment and a grotesque maternal body-evaluation.
jokes.explanation: A peak Selina-Catherine moment. 'Do you recognize her?' implies Catherine was unrecognizable due to weight, which is immediately confirmed with 'she lost all of her baby fat' — Selina reducing her daughter's identity to a body transformation. The delivery as a positive introduction makes the cruelty complete. High character integration.

jokes.text: The Meyer campaign used the health records of dead children to target and appeal to voters who were recently bereaved parents. Jesus. It's a real doozy, huh?
jokes.setup: Mike breaks down and confesses the new data breach
jokes.punchline: 'It's a real doozy, huh?' — Mike's cheerful understatement for using dead children's health records for political targeting
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's darkest and funniest moments. 'A real doozy, huh?' applied to using dead children's health records is maximally inappropriate — and the folksy word 'doozy' makes it worse. Mike's reflex to minimize even this crosses a line so far it loops back to funny. Highly quotable and very Veep in its commitment to darkness.

jokes.text: Seems like the party has hit a quiet point.
jokes.setup: The room has gone silent after the revelation about dead children's health records
jokes.punchline: 'Seems like the party has hit a quiet point' — Tom James's masterful understatement of the room's collective horror
jokes.explanation: A brilliant line. 'The party has hit a quiet point' is the most civilized possible response to a political atrocity being revealed — Tom James processes horror with the cadence of a dinner guest commenting on a lull in conversation. Near-perfect economy (nine words) and completely character-specific to Tom James's composed political persona. Among the episode's top five lines.

jokes.text: It's not really as bad as it sounds. You know, those kids have been dead for a while.
jokes.setup: The room is reeling from the dead-children data breach revelation
jokes.punchline: 'Those kids have been dead for a while' as the mitigating factor — as if the passage of time makes exploiting dead children's records less objectionable
jokes.explanation: The single darkest and funniest line of the episode, and one of the best lines in Veep's run. 'Those kids have been dead for a while' as damage control is a perfect encapsulation of political amorality — the duration of death as a mitigating legal and ethical factor. It's also structurally perfect: 'it's not as bad as it sounds' (sets up reassurance) followed by the most inadequate reassurance possible. Could only be Selina. Maximum character integration. This approaches the 9.5 anchor.

jokes.text: He's really good. It's because there's seven of us. I meant as a politician. I know. Yeah, it's... yeah.
jokes.setup: Someone says Tom James is 'really good' and Jonah mishears it as a reference to the group size
jokes.punchline: Jonah thinks 'he's really good' refers to Tom James's counting of people in the room, not his political skill — followed by Richard's diplomatic 'I know. Yeah.'
jokes.explanation: Jonah's mishearing is a classic bit of willful or genuine stupidity. Richard's 'I know. Yeah, it's... yeah.' is a masterclass in diplomatic agreement with someone who is wrong — he neither corrects nor confirms.

jokes.text: Did you see Tom James on Meet the Press? Oh, yeah. I'd like to press his meat. He's sexy without knowing he's sexy, and that's really sexy.
jokes.setup: The women are discussing Tom James's TV appearance
jokes.punchline: 'I'd like to press his meat' — a reverse wordplay on 'Meet the Press,' then the meta-analysis of his unconscious sexiness
jokes.explanation: 'Press his meat' is an obvious reversal of 'Meet the Press' — the wordplay is functional but not particularly inventive. The triple use of 'sexy' in one sentence is an annoying quality of the delivery but captures character.

jokes.text: It's really difficult talking to you now that you're sober. That's tough.
jokes.setup: Selina's sober friend has been earnestly critical all night
jokes.punchline: The admission that sobriety makes someone harder to talk to — because they're honest — is a dark inversion of conventional wisdom about substance abuse
jokes.explanation: A neat reversal — the sober person is the problem because honesty is unwelcome. Veep's cynical view that social functioning requires disinhibition is consistent here.

jokes.text: You're eating ice cream? Really? She's eating ice cream, Gary? I mean... I just had a bite.
jokes.setup: The dinner is winding down and Selina has apparently started eating ice cream
jokes.punchline: The visible ice cream eating is treated as a transgression by departing guests — Selina's response ('I just had a bite') is instinctively defensive
jokes.explanation: A callback to the tub of ice cream promise. The visual of the president being caught eating ice cream by departing guests is characterful, though the laugh is more situational than verbal.

jokes.text: Ma'am, intruder in the grounds. God, another one? You've got to be shitting me. No, Gary, it's the same one. He's doing a victory lap.
jokes.setup: A second intruder alert goes out just as the evening is ending
jokes.punchline: 'It's the same one. He's doing a victory lap.' — the intruder has returned, framed as an athletic achievement
jokes.explanation: 'He's doing a victory lap' is an outstanding line. The frame shift from security threat to sporting achievement — the intruder as an athlete completing his circuit — is both original and sharply economical. Highly quotable and a perfect callback to the day's events.

jokes.text: I shook Jack Nicholson's hand and I thought, 'If this hand could talk, it would say wash me.' Right, 'cause of all the sex stuff.
jokes.setup: Someone shares an anecdote about shaking Jack Nicholson's hand
jokes.punchline: 'Wash me' — the hand's implied hygiene issue based on Nicholson's sexual reputation — followed by Richard clarifying 'because of all the sex stuff'
jokes.explanation: A good layered joke. The 'wash me' image is original and specific. Richard's clarification — 'right, 'cause of all the sex stuff' — is the topper, his earnest explanation of an innuendo that didn't need explaining.

jokes.text: They're just gonna keep coming and coming until they kill us. This is classic copycat. Yeah, no, it's classic copycat. Ah, it's just like me just then. You know, it's classic copycat.
jokes.setup: The second intruder alert triggers another lockdown
jokes.punchline: Someone says 'it's classic copycat,' and another person says 'just like me just then' — i.e., they themselves just repeated 'classic copycat,' making them the copycat, then continues to say 'classic copycat' again
jokes.explanation: A quietly clever meta-joke — the person noting copycat behavior is themselves copying, and then copies again by continuing to say 'classic copycat.' The self-referential loop is subtle and rewards attention.

jokes.text: What time is it? It's stop... raffle time. Get those tickets out! The lovely Kimberly over here is gonna pull the winning ticket out of the cement mixer, which I thought was a nice touch.
jokes.setup: The concrete lobbying event is apparently running a raffle
jokes.punchline: 'Pull the winning ticket out of the cement mixer' — the raffle vessel is a cement mixer, the lobbying event fully committed to its concrete theme in the most literal way possible
jokes.explanation: A nice visual gag — the cement mixer as raffle vessel is so on-the-nose for the concrete industry that it loops back to funny. 'Which I thought was a nice touch' as self-congratulation is the extra beat.

jokes.text: Is lobbying always like this? I have a funny sinking feeling it might be.
jokes.setup: The raffle is underway at the lobby event
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'funny sinking feeling' — 'funny' is the wrong word, 'sinking' captures the existential horror, and the formulation together reflects Amy's dark self-awareness about her new career
jokes.explanation: Amy's self-aware horror at her own career trajectory is strong character comedy. 'Funny sinking feeling' as a phrase is slightly oxymoronic in a way that suits the scene.

jokes.text: I wish the intruder would just kill me. Well, if the intruder kills you, Mike, then who's gonna announce your death?
jokes.setup: Mike is having a crisis of professional confidence at the lobbying event
jokes.punchline: The response to Mike's death wish is purely professional — who would announce his death? — treating his hypothetical death as a communications problem
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. 'Who's gonna announce your death?' is the perfect response to a press secretary's death wish — it reframes his death as a communications problem rather than a human tragedy. Character-specific to Mike, structurally clean, maximally dark, and highly quotable. One of the episode's top-tier moments.

jokes.text: Your face looks kind of like a sad egg.
jokes.setup: Mike is visibly distressed
jokes.punchline: 'A sad egg' — the image of Mike's face as a sad egg is both absurdly specific and visually apt
jokes.explanation: 'Sad egg' is a genuinely original insult/observation. The simplicity of the image — oval, expressionless, round — captures something true about Mike's face in crisis. Maximum economy (three words) for a substantial laugh.

jokes.text: I think I'm a fraud. I'm not good at my job. The story about the emperor and the new clothes. I'm the emperor. No, I'm the clothes. I'm the guy who will fuck this government.
jokes.setup: Mike is having an existential breakdown about his professional inadequacy
jokes.punchline: He tries to apply the Emperor's New Clothes allegory to himself and gets it backwards — then gives up and settles on the wrong version, then abandons the metaphor entirely
jokes.explanation: A brilliant Mike moment. His attempt to use the Emperor's New Clothes as a self-insight metaphor breaks down in real time — he can't even correctly identify which character he is. 'No, I'm the clothes' is the pivot, and then his final surrender ('I'm the guy who will fuck this government') abandons allegory for raw despair. Layers of character comedy — the analytical breakdown, the self-awareness that isn't quite self-aware enough, the eventual confession.

jokes.text: Hey, at least you got your hair.
jokes.setup: Mike is having a complete existential breakdown about his career
jokes.punchline: 'At least you got your hair' — the most minimal possible consolation that accidentally implies everything else is lost
jokes.explanation: A perfectly economical consolation that is also a total dismissal. By praising only his hair, the speaker confirms that nothing else is praiseworthy. The timing (following Mike's complete breakdown) makes it devastating and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I can't breathe. I can't breathe. I can't breathe. Oh, honey. You need water. Can somebody help her? These people are only here to protect me, I'm afraid.
jokes.setup: One of Selina's friends is having a panic attack during the intruder lockdown
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to her friend's panic attack — 'these people are only here to protect me' — prioritizing her own security detail over her friend's medical emergency
jokes.explanation: Selina's narcissism applied to a genuine medical emergency — she explains the Secret Service's purpose not to be helpful but to clarify her own importance. 'I'm afraid' as a polite qualifier after the most self-centered response possible is the perfect touch.

jokes.text: The only person who's interested in killing you, Deborah, is here in this room. Ahem, that was a joke. You know, you can't see my face, but I'm actually making a joke. Put that back. Put it back.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to comfort her friend by making a joke about the intruder threat
jokes.punchline: The joke lands so badly (implying Selina herself wants to kill Deborah) that Selina has to explain it's a joke — twice — while apparently someone has picked up a weapon
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed ending joke. Selina's joke is genuinely threatening (she IS in the room), the explanation of the joke ('you can't see my face, but I'm making a joke') is funnier than the joke itself, and the 'put it back' suggests someone has actually reached for a weapon in response. Layers of comedy: the dark joke, the meta-explanation, the physical escalation. A strong episode closer.

tmdb_overview: The president is shaken by a dramatic incident at the White House.
insight: Family dynamics episode trades Veep's usual pace for character depth, landing just below excellence.
